output:
# MONOMARGIN and CALIPER: Treating the Counterfactual Normality Margin as a Per-Graph Calibrated Field instead of a Global Perturbation Budget in Graph-Level Anomaly Detection

A research-idea proposal in the 5-question (5q) format. The proposal reframes the perturbation budget / corruption strength used by counterfactual-contrastive and one-class graph-level anomaly detectors from a single GLOBAL CONSTANT into a per-graph, calibrated counterfactual-margin FIELD, while preserving a provable, zero-regression exact-collapse identity back to the deployed fixed-strength baseline. The reframing is designed to attack the field's most embarrassing pathology - the performance flip - at its mechanistic root.

Notation note: all symbols are plain ASCII. Greek letters are spelled out (alpha, gamma, eta, theta, lambda, mu, phi, rho, tau). Inequalities use "<=" and ">=". Em-dashes are written as " - ". Gradients are written grad, indicator functions as 1[.].

---

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 The graph-level anomaly detection problem

Graph-level anomaly detection (GLAD) seeks whole-graph instances that differ significantly from the majority of a graph population - abnormal molecules, disordered brain networks, fraudulent transaction subgraphs [1], [3]. Unlike node- or edge-level detection, the unit of scoring is an entire graph G with its topology and node/edge attributes, and the operative regime is one-class / unsupervised: a model is trained on graphs assumed (mostly) normal, with anomalies rare, unlabeled, or absent at training time [2], [5]. A detector learns a scoring function s_theta(G) (higher = more anomalous) and a decision threshold tau set on a normal-validation quantile.

### 1.2 The four method families

(i) One-class / hypersphere methods adapt Deep SVDD [20] to graphs: OCGIN [3] embeds graphs with a GIN [21] and minimizes the volume of a hypersphere enclosing normal embeddings; OCGTL [4] "raises the bar" by combining one-class classification with learned neural transformations to resist representation collapse. (ii) Knowledge-distillation methods: GLocalKD [2] trains a student to predict a random teacher's node- and graph-level representations, scoring anomalies by global-and-local distillation error. (iii) Memory / prototype methods: HimNet [5] stores hierarchical memory prototypes of normality and scores by reconstruction against node- and graph-level memories. (iv) Contrastive / generative / discriminative methods: GLAD via graph contrastive learning and out-of-distribution detection (GOOD-D [8]), spectral re-thinking of message passing for anomaly detection (BWGNN [9]), and the dual-discriminative imbalanced detector iGAD [6]. A parallel interpretability line, SIGNET [7], uses a multi-view subgraph information bottleneck to detect AND explain which substructure makes a graph anomalous.

### 1.3 The counterfactual and contrastive turn

The Introduction's reference list is dominated by counterfactual graph learning and graph contrastive learning, signposting the current research frontier. Counterfactual graph learning asks: what minimal edit to G would change its label? It has been used for node-level anomaly detection on attributed networks [10], for generating counterfactual hard-negative samples that sharpen graph contrastive learning [11], and most recently for GLAD directly: IGAD-CF [12] synthesizes counterfactual anomalies to rebalance the scarce-anomaly regime. The contrastive machinery these methods sit on top of - GraphCL [13] and its automated/adaptive descendants JOAO [14], GCA [15], and the adversarial AD-GCL [16] - manufactures a second "view" T_r(G) of each graph by an augmentation operator (edge drop/add, node/attribute masking, subgraph sampling) with a corruption strength r, and pulls a graph's two views together while pushing different graphs apart.

### 1.4 The pathology that haunts the field: the performance flip

Zhao and Akoglu [3] documented a disquieting property of GLAD benchmarks built from binary graph-classification datasets: which class one designates "normal" versus "anomaly" can invert detector accuracy. A method that scores AUC well above 0.5 when class A is normal can score well BELOW 0.5 - worse than random - when the assignment is flipped. The "performance flip" reveals that prevailing detectors do not measure intrinsic anomalousness; they measure conformity to whichever class is denser and lower-dispersion. This is the central unresolved validity problem of GLAD, and it is the problem this proposal targets.

### 1.5 Where the field stands

The state of the art has produced (a) strong one-class and distillation objectives (OCGIN, OCGTL, GLocalKD), (b) memory and discriminative refinements (HimNet, iGAD, BWGNN), (c) interpretable detection (SIGNET), and (d) a counterfactual-contrastive frontier (IGAD-CF, counterfactual hard negatives) sitting on contrastive augmentation (GraphCL, JOAO, GCA, AD-GCL). What it has NOT produced is any detector in which the perturbation budget / normality margin used to build counterfactual and contrastive views is conditioned on each graph's own measured distance to the normal-manifold boundary. The budget adapts to the dataset (JOAO), to node importance within a graph (GCA), or adversarially to the contrastive loss (AD-GCL), but it is still ONE corruption strength applied with the same magnitude to a graph sitting deep inside the normal region and to a graph hovering at its edge. This proposal targets exactly that unexamined assumption, and ties closing it to dissolving the performance flip.

---

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

This section states the analytical core that Section 3 formalizes. It names the gap (MONOMARGIN), the method (CALIPER), the design principle (exact collapse), and the novelty wedge (C1-C4).

### 2.1 Finding 1 - MONOMARGIN is a representational impossibility, not a tuning error

Counterfactual-contrastive and one-class GLAD share a hidden constant. The model carries an implicit normal manifold M_n = { G : s_theta(G) <= tau }, whose boundary is the level set s_theta = tau. Every graph G has a (latent, unobserved) NORMALITY MARGIN m(G): the minimal validity-preserving edit cost needed to move G across that boundary - the counterfactual flip radius. Training uses a perturbation operator T_r with a SINGLE GLOBAL BUDGET r_0 in three roles: as a label-preserving positive view (T_{r_0}(G) should stay on G's side), as a counterfactual hard negative (a push intended to cross the boundary [11], [12]), and implicitly as the unit in which the anomaly score is read. We name this gap MONOMARGIN: a Monolithic, graph-agnostic margin - one corruption budget r_0 applied identically to all graphs, blind to the dispersion of { m(G) }.

The defining claim is that MONOMARGIN is a REPRESENTATIONAL impossibility. When the population of margins { m(G) } is dispersed - which is exactly when a population contains both prototypical and borderline normals - NO single r_0 can be simultaneously correct:

- For a deep-interior normal graph (large m), r_0 is too small. The "counterfactual negative" T_{r_0}(G) never crosses the boundary; it remains normal but is labeled anomalous - a FALSE NEGATIVE injected into the discriminative/contrastive objective.
- For a near-boundary normal graph (small m), r_0 is too large. The "positive augmentation" T_{r_0}(G) crosses the boundary; it is now anomalous but is treated as a same-class positive - a FALSE POSITIVE that pulls an anomalous view toward normal and erodes the very boundary the detector is trying to learn.

You cannot tune r_0 out of this: lowering it fixes the near-boundary graphs and breaks the interior ones, raising it does the reverse. The error is structural because the correct budget is a FUNCTION of an unobserved per-graph quantity m(G), and a constant cannot be that function.

### 2.2 Finding 2 - MONOMARGIN is the mechanism behind the performance flip

The two error modes are ASYMMETRIC in a way that explains [3]. Near-boundary mass is created by the high-dispersion class. When the high-dispersion class is designated normal, near-boundary false positives dominate, the learned boundary is dragged outward, and the score collapses toward measuring class density rather than anomalousness - producing AUC below 0.5 when the assignment is flipped. MONOMARGIN thus predicts the flip as a downstream symptom: a budget that ignores per-graph margin necessarily bakes in the class-density asymmetry that Zhao and Akoglu observed. A method that calibrates the budget to each graph's actual margin attacks the mechanism, not the symptom - which is why we elevate the flip-gap to a primary metric (O4, Q5).

### 2.3 Finding 3 - the margin can be estimated FOR FREE, and that is the unlock

It LOOKS as if conditioning on m(G) requires knowing anomaly labels (to know where the boundary is) - the very thing GLAD lacks. The unlock is that the local distance-to-boundary is a first-order geometric byproduct of the detector itself. For a smooth score, the signed distance from G to the level set { s_theta = tau } is approximated by the DeepFool-style estimate (s_theta(G) - tau) / || grad s_theta(G) || [17], computed in a relaxed (continuous) graph-edit space - a single extra backward pass. We triangulate this self-channel with two non-self channels - a frozen teacher's score sensitivity to a probe perturbation, and embedding distance to the k nearest normal prototypes / memories (HimNet-style [5]) - and pass the raw estimate through a calibration map fit on a NORMAL-ONLY probe, where the true flip radius is measured by binary search on r until the frozen teacher's score crosses its own normal quantile. No anomaly labels are required at any point.

### 2.4 The method: CALIPER

We introduce CALIPER (CALIbrated PER-graph counterfactual margin), which promotes the budget scalar to a field and acts on it with two orthogonal knobs. The free estimate m_hat(G) = aggregate( e1 score-gradient distance, e2 frozen-teacher sensitivity, e3 prototype/memory distance ) is calibrated to m_cal(G) = c(m_hat(G)) by isotonic/Platt regression [18], [19] on the normal-only flip-radius probe, anchored so that a constant input maps to a constant output. KNOB1 (selection / routing): g(G) = 1[ m_cal(G) >= gamma ] decides which graphs may contribute a base-strength positive view (deep-interior graphs), routing near-boundary graphs out of the positive set and, optionally, into the counterfactual-negative set. KNOB2 (per-graph budget + soft counterfactual-relabel): the budget becomes r(G) = rho(m_cal(G)) (monotone non-decreasing; larger margin -> larger safe budget), the loss weight w(G) = phi(m_cal(G)) (monotone non-decreasing, phi at the anchor = 1), and the view's training target is SOFT-relabeled by the calibrated probability p_flip(G, r) that T_r(G) crossed the boundary - a view counts as a mixture of "positive" and "counterfactual negative" weighted by p_flip, instead of a hard same-class positive. The CALIPER objective is

  L_CALIPER = sum_G  g(G) * w(G) * ell( s_theta ; G, T_{r(G)}(G), p_flip(G, r(G)) ),

where ell is the underlying contrastive/one-class/discriminative loss of any base detector. At inference, the anomaly score is the calibrated counterfactual cost m_cal(G) itself (a symmetric, geodesic-like distance to normality), reported alongside the base score for non-inferiority.

### 2.5 The decisive design property: EXACT-COLLAPSE

EXACT-COLLAPSE is a DESIGN SWITCH that disables estimation, not a claim that the true margin is constant. Set m_hat(G) = m_0 for all G. Then m_cal(G) = c(m_0) = constant, so g(G) = 1[constant >= gamma] = 1 for any gamma <= constant, r(G) = rho(constant) = r_0, w(G) = phi(constant) = 1, and p_flip reduces to the base method's hard same-class assignment. Term by term, L_CALIPER == L_base sample-for-sample, with per-sample discrepancy E_collapse = max_G | g(G) w(G) ell(.) - ell_base(.) | <= 1e-6 enforced by a CI-gateable build check (1e-6 is a finite-precision budget, not a performance claim). CALIPER is therefore a strict, safe generalization: zero regression at the identity default.

### 2.6 The novelty wedge (no prior line covers more than two clauses)

- C1 - the normality margin / perturbation budget is modeled as a per-graph FIELD m(G), not a global constant (scalar -> field promotion).
- C2 - the field is estimated FOR FREE from signals the detector already produces (e1 score-gradient distance [17], e2 frozen-teacher sensitivity, e3 prototype/memory distance), with NO anomaly labels, then passed through a calibration map c(.) fit on a normal-only flip-radius probe.
- C3 - TWO orthogonal knobs act on the SAME calibrated field: a selection gate (gamma) routing graphs to positive vs counterfactual-negative roles, and a per-graph budget + soft counterfactual-relabel (rho, phi, p_flip).
- C4 - an EXACT-COLLAPSE strict generalization with a CI-gateable E_collapse <= 1e-6 back to fixed-strength counterfactual-contrastive / one-class GLAD (zero regression at the identity default).

### 2.7 Boundary recoveries (named baseline => exact special point of the field) [provable-by-construction]

- R1 No-augmentation / pure one-class: gamma > max_G m_cal(G) => g(G) = 0 for all augmented positives => train on raw normals only (Deep SVDD / OCGIN regime).
- R2 Vanilla fixed-strength counterfactual-contrastive / one-class GLAD: m constant => r = r_0, hard labels => L_CALIPER == L_base. This is the deployed baseline and the COLLAPSE TARGET (GraphCL / IGAD-CF / GLocalKD-style fixed strength).
- R3 Pure selection / curriculum (KNOB2 off): rho == r_0, p_flip hard => objective = sum_G g(G) * ell_base (gate-only admission of deep-interior graphs).
- R4 Pure soft-relabel / reweight (KNOB1 off): gamma = 0 => g(G) = 1 for all G; only r(G), w(G), p_flip act (per-graph budget + soft relabel only).
- R5 Oracle margin (ceiling): m_hat replaced by the true binary-search flip radius under the frozen teacher on a labeled-validity probe.

### 2.8 Load-bearing symbols

G graph; s_theta(G) anomaly score; tau normal-quantile threshold; M_n normal manifold; m(G) true normality margin / flip radius; T_r(G) validity-preserving perturbation with edit budget r; r_0 global budget (MONOMARGIN); m_hat(G) free margin estimate; e1/e2/e3 estimator channels; c(.) calibration map; m_cal(G) = c(m_hat(G)); gamma gate threshold; g(G) gate; rho budget map; r(G) = rho(m_cal(G)) per-graph budget; phi weight map; w(G) loss weight; p_flip(G, r) calibrated boundary-crossing probability; ell base per-sample loss; L_base deployed objective; L_CALIPER the CALIPER objective; E_collapse exact-collapse budget; epsilon do-no-harm margin; keep_rate fraction of graphs admitted as positives; flip_gap = | AUC(A normal) - (1 - AUC(B normal)) |; identity default = disable estimation (m_hat(G) = m_0 for all G).

---

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Does promoting the perturbation budget / normality margin in counterfactual-contrastive graph-level anomaly detection from a single global constant to a per-graph calibrated counterfactual-margin field m(G) - estimated for free from the detector's own score-gradient geometry, a frozen-teacher sensitivity, and prototype distance, calibrated on a normal-only flip-radius probe, and used to gate and soft-counterfactually-relabel training views - reduce the performance-flip gap and improve graph-level anomaly detection without regressing at the identity default, relative to fixed-strength baselines?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

P1 (the status quo it disrupts: a global perturbation budget). The counterfactual-contrastive frontier of GLAD - counterfactual hard negatives [11], counterfactual anomaly synthesis IGAD-CF [12], sitting on contrastive augmentation GraphCL [13], JOAO [14], GCA [15], AD-GCL [16] - and the one-class line it competes with (OCGIN [3], OCGTL [4], GLocalKD [2], HimNet [5]) all manufacture views with ONE corruption strength r_0, silently asserting that every graph is equidistant from the normal-manifold boundary. CALIPER disrupts exactly this unexamined constant: MONOMARGIN asserts the margin is a heterogeneous per-graph field, and the deployed objective L_base is merely the constant-margin special case of a margin-conditioned objective. The community has been optimizing a corner of a richer objective without naming the corner.

P2 (the silent, asymmetric harm: mislabeled views that drive the flip). A global budget injects two opposite errors - false-negative "counterfactuals" that never cross the boundary for interior graphs, and false-positive "positives" that do cross it for borderline graphs. These are a first-order data-quality failure in exactly the regime (one-class, no anomaly labels) where there is no corrective signal. Crucially the harm is ASYMMETRIC and CONCENTRATED at the near-boundary tail, which is precisely the structure that produces the performance flip [3] - the field's most cited validity embarrassment. The detector already has every signal needed to detect the margin (its own score, gradient, prototypes) yet feeds none of it back: a measured-but-not-fed-back gap.

P3 (why it advances knowledge: an unmeasured assumption becomes a calibrated, inspectable field). The contribution is epistemic before it is empirical. CALIPER turns the unmeasured assumption (margin == r_0 everywhere) into a calibrated, inspectable per-graph quantity m_cal(G), and reframes the community's question from "which augmenter / objective is best?" to "WHERE is each graph relative to the normal boundary?" - a per-graph, stratifiable question. It yields a reusable MARGIN-PROBE protocol (AUROC/ECE of m_cal against measured flip radius) that any GLAD method can be audited with, and a principled, mechanism-level account of the performance flip rather than a benchmark caveat. Naming m(G) and measuring it is the durable knowledge; the two knobs are the first thing one does once the field exists.

P4 (practical payoff: cheap, detector-agnostic, drop-in, risk-free). The estimator signals are cheap: e1 is one extra backward pass [17], e3 is a nearest-prototype lookup the memory methods already compute [5], and the probe that fits c(.) is small and offline. CALIPER is therefore detector-agnostic (it wraps OCGIN, OCGTL, GLocalKD, HimNet, iGAD, GraphCL-style contrastive heads) and backbone-agnostic (GIN [21], GCN [22], DiffPool [23]) and drops into a pipeline as a per-graph gate g(G) and budget r(G). Adoption is risk-free by construction: disabling estimation (m_hat(G) = m_0) reproduces the deployed baseline sample-for-sample with E_collapse <= 1e-6, removing the usual barrier to touching a working detector.

P5 (the stakes: validity and safety). GLAD is deployed where false confidence is costly - flagging toxic molecules, screening brain-network disorders, triaging fraud [1]. The performance flip means a detector that looks excellent on a benchmark can be worse-than-random under a different-but-equally-valid normal/anomaly convention - a latent reliability hazard. Dissolving the flip at its mechanistic root converts GLAD scores from "conformity to the dominant class" into "calibrated distance to normality," which is the property practitioners actually need. CALIPER targets the dominant validity axis of the field, at the metric the community most distrusts, for nearly free, with a provable safety net.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

O1 CIRCULARITY [REPRESENTATIONAL]. The free self-channel e1 uses the detector's own score-gradient to estimate the margin that then trains the detector. NAIVE FAILS: a detector with a flat/collapsed score region (a known one-class failure mode, the very thing OCGTL [4] was built to resist) reports a huge margin, the gate admits it, and the budget over-perturbs it - manufacturing exactly the false-positive views MONOMARGIN warns about; you cannot threshold your way out of a contaminated signal. REPRESENTATIONAL because the fix is a structural choice about WHERE the signal comes from. MITIGATION: anchor m_hat on non-self channels (e3 prototype distance, e2 a FROZEN or cross-fit teacher), never the live theta; calibrate against the external probe. Falsified by the PLACEBO rung; bounded by the ORACLE rung.

O2 CALIBRATION WITHOUT ANOMALY LABELS [REPRESENTATIONAL]. KNOB1's gamma and KNOB2's p_flip need m_cal(G) to behave like a true flip radius, but GLAD has no anomaly labels at train time. NAIVE FAILS: raw detector confidence is miscalibrated and overconfident, so thresholding it conflates "the detector is sure" with "the graph is far from the boundary." REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces the calibration map c(.) into the architecture - m_hat is never used directly. MITIGATION: fit c(.) on a NORMAL-ONLY probe where the flip radius is measured by binary search on r until the frozen teacher's score crosses its own normal quantile (no anomalies needed); anchor c at the identity point so EXACT-COLLAPSE holds; report ECE(m_cal) far below ECE(raw m_hat) [18], [19]. The LEAKAGE rung keeps the probe disjoint and frozen.

O3 CONFOUNDING KEEP-RATE WITH MARGIN [REPRESENTATIONAL]. KNOB1 changes the SIZE and DIVERSITY of the positive set, and size alone changes generalization. NAIVE FAILS: a "we gated and improved" result cannot separate gains from MEASURING the margin (the claim) from gains from merely training on fewer/easier graphs (a confound). REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces a control into the design - keep_rate becomes a first-class reported quantity and the placebo is built to match it. MITIGATION: a rate-matched random-field PLACEBO at the SAME keep_rate; report keep_rate with every AUC; PASS requires CALIPER > placebo at matched keep_rate beyond seed noise.

O4 THE PERFORMANCE-FLIP STRESS TEST [DOMAIN-SPECIFIC; the headline]. A method that improves AUC under one normal/anomaly assignment but not its flip has NOT fixed the pathology - it has merely picked the lucky side. NAIVE FAILS: reporting single-assignment AUC hides the flip [3]. MITIGATION: evaluate under BOTH class-as-normal assignments and make flip_gap = | AUC(A normal) - (1 - AUC(B normal)) | a PRIMARY readout; PASS requires CALIPER to SHRINK flip_gap versus every baseline, not just to raise one-sided AUC.

O5 PERTURBATION SEMANTICS / VALIDITY [DOMAIN-SPECIFIC]. A "margin in edit space" is meaningful only if edits produce VALID graphs; an off-manifold molecule with impossible valence is not a counterfactual, it is noise. NAIVE FAILS: unconstrained edge/attribute edits measure distance in a space the data manifold does not occupy. MITIGATION: restrict T_r to a validity-preserving edit set (valence-checked edits for molecules, degree-preserving rewiring otherwise), measure margin in the validity-constrained edit metric, and report the validity rate of generated counterfactuals as a gating diagnostic.

O6 METRIC BLINDNESS [REPRESENTATIONAL]. Pooled AUC averages over the whole population; the near-boundary tail where MONOMARGIN concentrates is a minority and barely moves the mean. NAIVE FAILS: a pooled number hides both the harm and the fix. REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces the evaluation's structure - the primary readout must be stratified by margin, since the method's value is the GRADIENT of lift across margin deciles. MITIGATION: stratified AUC by m_cal decile as PRIMARY, pooled AUC reported only for non-inferiority.

O7 ESTIMATOR/PROBE COST AND SCALABILITY [auxiliary]. e1 needs a backward pass and the probe needs binary-search rollouts. NAIVE FAILS: if the field costs as much as retraining, "for free" is false. MITIGATION: e1 is one extra backward; e3 reuses memory lookups [5]; the probe is small and offline; report wall-clock overhead vs baseline as evidence the field is cheap.

WHY IT IS NONETHELESS TRACTABLE. Every obstacle is matched by a structural feature already in the frame: circularity (O1) is fenced by non-self channels (e2, e3) plus a frozen/cross-fit teacher; the missing-label problem (O2) is solved by a normal-only flip-radius probe rather than anomaly labels; the keep-rate confound (O3) and metric blindness (O6) are neutralized by the rate-matched PLACEBO and the mandated stratified readout; semantics (O5) is handled by validity-constrained edits; and the flip stress test (O4) turns the field's worst pathology into the primary success metric. Above all, EXACT-COLLAPSE (set m_hat = m_0 => L_CALIPER == L_base, E_collapse <= 1e-6) pins the downside to zero, so the entire research risk is on the UPSIDE.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior art falls into five families; for each we give representative work, what it does, the precise delta CALIPER adds, and the boundary recovery it maps to.

(1) Counterfactual / generative GLAD - counterfactual hard negatives [11], IGAD-CF [12], node-level counterfactual anomaly detection [10]. WHAT: synthesize counterfactual anomalies or hard negatives to enrich/rebalance training. DELTA: they generate counterfactuals at a fixed or adversarial GLOBAL strength and never model the per-graph flip radius as a calibrated field, nor feed it back as a gate + soft relabel, nor offer an exact-collapse identity. They map to R2 (constant margin). CALIPER adds the full wedge C1-C4 on top of any such generator.

(2) Contrastive augmentation - GraphCL [13], JOAO [14], GCA [15], AD-GCL [16]. WHAT: build positive views by augmentation; JOAO learns a DATASET-level augmentation distribution, GCA weights NODE/EDGE importance WITHIN a graph, AD-GCL learns an ADVERSARIAL edge-dropper to minimize redundant information. DELTA: none conditions the corruption strength on a per-graph, calibrated distance-to-anomaly-boundary; GCA adapts within a graph but not to the graph's margin, JOAO adapts across the dataset but applies one policy to all graphs, AD-GCL optimizes a contrastive (not anomaly-margin) criterion and has no normal-only calibration or collapse identity. They map to R2/R4. CALIPER's field is anomaly-margin-calibrated, gated, and collapse-safe.

(3) One-class / distillation / memory GLAD - OCGIN [3], OCGTL [4], GLocalKD [2], HimNet [5], Deep SVDD [20]. WHAT: learn a compact normal representation and score by distance/reconstruction. DELTA: they emit a SINGLE global score with a global threshold tau and never expose or exploit the per-graph margin; their own geometry (gradient, prototype distance) is the free signal they leave unused. They map to R1/R2. CALIPER reads the margin OUT of their geometry and acts on it.

(4) Performance-flip / benchmark-validity analyses - Zhao and Akoglu [3]. WHAT: DIAGNOSE the flip and warn against classification-derived benchmarks. DELTA: it is a diagnosis, not a method; it offers no per-graph mechanism and no fix. CALIPER supplies the mechanistic account (asymmetric near-boundary mislabeling under a constant budget) AND the remedy (a calibrated margin field), and elevates flip_gap to a primary metric.

(5) Interpretable / subgraph GLAD - SIGNET [7], BWGNN [9]. WHAT: explain which substructure is anomalous (SIGNET) or re-think message passing spectrally (BWGNN). DELTA: SIGNET explains WHERE within a graph the anomaly is but still scores with a global criterion and does not model per-graph margin or the flip; BWGNN changes the encoder, not the budget. They are orthogonal and COMPOSABLE with CALIPER (its margin field can wrap their encoders).

THE TWO BARRIERS THAT KEPT IT UNSOLVED. (a) It LOOKED like it needed anomaly labels: knowing each graph's distance to the boundary appears to require knowing where the boundary is, i.e., labeled anomalies, which GLAD lacks. The UNLOCK is that the local distance-to-boundary is a first-order byproduct of the detector's own score geometry [17], anchored by prototype distance and a frozen teacher, and the calibration needs only a NORMAL-ONLY flip-radius probe - no anomaly labels. (b) The augmentation community (which controls the budget) and the anomaly-validity community (which named the flip) never met: budget adaptivity was studied for CONTRASTIVE quality (JOAO, GCA, AD-GCL) while the flip was studied as a BENCHMARK artifact [3], so no one placed a calibrated anomaly-margin field at the center of the budget with an exact-collapse safety net. CALIPER unifies them.

ONE CLEAN NOVELTY SENTENCE. CALIPER is the first GLAD method to model the per-graph counterfactual normality margin as a calibrated, free-to-estimate field, to act on it with BOTH a selection gate and a per-graph budget + soft counterfactual-relabel, and to provably collapse to fixed-strength counterfactual-contrastive / one-class detection at the identity default (E_collapse <= 1e-6) - while making the performance-flip gap a primary, mechanism-level target.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

METHOD PIPELINE (end-to-end). Step 0 Base detector: pick any base GLAD loss ell (OCGIN [3] hypersphere, OCGTL [4] transformations, GLocalKD [2] distillation, HimNet [5] memory, or a GraphCL-style contrastive head [13]); train to obtain s_theta and tau on a normal-validation quantile. Step 1 Estimate m_hat(G) for free from e1 score-gradient distance (s_theta(G) - tau)/|| grad s_theta(G) || in a relaxed validity-constrained edit space [17], e2 a FROZEN/cross-fit teacher's score sensitivity to a probe perturbation, and e3 distance to the k nearest normal prototypes/memories [5]. Step 2 Calibrate: m_cal(G) = c(m_hat(G)) with c fit by isotonic/Platt [18], [19] on a NORMAL-ONLY probe whose flip radius is measured by binary search on r against the frozen teacher's normal quantile; anchor c at the identity point. Step 3 KNOB1 gate: g(G) = 1[ m_cal(G) >= gamma ] routes deep-interior graphs to base-strength positives and near-boundary graphs out (or into the counterfactual-negative set). Step 4 KNOB2 budget + soft relabel: r(G) = rho(m_cal(G)), w(G) = phi(m_cal(G)), and the view target is a p_flip(G, r(G))-weighted mix of positive and counterfactual-negative. Step 5 Train on L_CALIPER = sum_G g(G) w(G) ell(s_theta ; G, T_{r(G)}(G), p_flip). Safety rails: frozen/cross-fit teacher (O1), validity-constrained edits (O5), do-no-harm gate (epsilon), and the EXACT-COLLAPSE check (set m_hat = m_0 => L_CALIPER == L_base, E_collapse <= 1e-6).

DATASETS / BENCHMARKS (all standard). The GLAD evaluation suites used by GLocalKD/OCGTL/HimNet/SIGNET on TUDataset [24] - molecular and bioinformatics graphs (PROTEINS, DD, NCI1, AIDS, ENZYMES, MUTAG, COX2, BZR, DHFR, OGB-style molecular sets) and social graphs (IMDB, REDDIT, COLLAB) - under the one-class protocol (train on one class as normal). CRITICALLY, the Zhao-Akoglu performance-flip protocol [3]: every dataset is run under BOTH class-as-normal assignments. Plus real anomaly tasks (molecular toxicity, brain-network disorder graphs) for external validity.

BACKBONES (field is backbone-agnostic): GIN [21], GCN [22], DiffPool [23]; teacher = frozen OCGIN [3] or HimNet memory [5].

BASELINES = boundary recoveries R1..R5 + strong competitors. R1 no-aug one-class; R2 fixed-strength counterfactual-contrastive / one-class (GraphCL [13] / IGAD-CF [12] / GLocalKD [2] strength) - the COLLAPSE TARGET; R3 gate-only; R4 reweight/soft-relabel-only; R5 oracle margin (ceiling); PLUS OCGTL [4], HimNet [5], iGAD [6], SIGNET [7], GOOD-D [8], BWGNN [9], AD-GCL [16] as external competitors to show CALIPER is additive on top of any base detector.

METRICS. Graph-level AUC-ROC and AUC-PR, POOLED and STRATIFIED by m_cal decile (stratified is PRIMARY); flip_gap = | AUC(A normal) - (1 - AUC(B normal)) | as a PRIMARY validity metric; calibration of the field, AUROC/ECE of m_cal vs measured flip radius (and raw vs calibrated, to show the lift) [18], [19]; keep_rate reported with every AUC (rate-matched placebo); counterfactual validity rate (O5); compute overhead vs baseline (to evidence "for free"); and E_collapse (CI-gateable, required <= 1e-6).

ABLATION GRID. Orthogonal 2x2 {KNOB1 on/off} x {KNOB2 on/off} (recovers R2, R3, R4, full CALIPER at the corners); estimator channels {e1, e2, e3, and all combos} x {raw vs calibrated}; gamma sweep (endpoints recover R1 gate-all-out and R2 gate-all-in); rho budget-shape sweep; p_flip soft-relabel sweep (conservative -> aggressive); teacher source {in-loop theta, frozen snapshot, cross-fit} to expose circularity (O1).

FALSIFIER-LADDER RUNS (explicit PASS conditions). ORACLE (R5): replace m_hat with the true flip radius - PASS if baseline < CALIPER <= oracle AND the oracle shows real headroom; FALSIFIES the estimator if free m_hat is no better than PLACEBO. PLACEBO (rate-matched random field): PASS if CALIPER > placebo beyond seed noise at matched keep_rate; FALSIFIES if the gain was merely dropping data. RECOVERY: disable estimation (m_hat = m_0) - PASS if L_CALIPER == L_base sample-for-sample, E_collapse <= 1e-6; FALSIFIES on any deviation > 1e-6. LEAKAGE: probe disjoint from eval, m_hat never reads eval labels, calibration split frozen - PASS if gains survive the strict split. FLIP-INVARIANCE (O4): both class-as-normal assignments - PASS if flip_gap shrinks vs every baseline. DO-NO-HARM: near-constant-margin / single-assignment regime - PASS if pooled AUC >= baseline - epsilon on every cell.

EXPECTED OUTCOMES (as HYPOTHESES; provable items flagged). H1 [conjectured->measured]: a MODEST pooled-AUC lift over R2 but a LARGE reduction in flip_gap and a LARGE lift at the near-boundary (low-m_cal) deciles - the GRADIENT of lift across deciles is itself the evidence that MONOMARGIN is real. H2 [conjectured->measured]: both knobs contribute - KNOB1 removes outright mislabeled views, KNOB2 recovers borderline mass a hard gate would waste; full CALIPER > R3 and > R4. H3 [conjectured->measured]: e3 (prototype distance) + e1 (score-gradient) calibrated is best; e1 alone is most circularity-prone. H4 [conjectured->measured]: calibrated m_cal achieves AUROC well above 0.5 against measured flip radius with ECE far below raw confidence. H5 [conjectured->measured]: a frozen/cross-fit teacher beats an in-loop teacher - direct confirmation that circularity (O1) is real, not a strawman. H6 [conjectured->measured]: free m_hat approaches the ORACLE (R5) ceiling and clearly beats the rate-matched PLACEBO. P1 [provable-by-construction]: EXACT-COLLAPSE - set m_hat = m_0 => L_CALIPER == L_base, E_collapse <= 1e-6. P2 [provable-by-construction]: boundary recoveries R1..R5 are exact special points of the field. P3 [provable-by-construction underwriting a conjectured->measured outcome]: DO-NO-HARM within epsilon at the identity/near-identity default, underwritten by P1 (P1 holds under exact arithmetic; empirical near-identity behavior under finite precision and approximate calibration is a separate, measured claim).

RESPONSIBLE-AI AND DEPLOYMENT CONSIDERATIONS. (1) Decision-support framing: CALIPER's rails (KNOB1 gate, DO-NO-HARM epsilon, EXACT-COLLAPSE) assume m_cal is faithful, so it is positioned as decision support, not an autonomous gate - a human spot-check of low-m_cal (near-boundary) flags is required before high-stakes use (toxicity, clinical brain networks). (2) Confidently-wrong margins: a c(.) fit on easy normals can over-estimate m_cal on distribution-shift graphs (where the flip is worst), so we recommend a conservative (high) gamma on first deployment and report ECE of m_cal on a held-out shifted subset separately. (3) Probe governance: the normal-only flip-radius probe defines "normal"; we document its construction, release it for independent calibration audit, and report sensitivity of results to the probe quantile. (4) Benchmark honesty: flip_gap and keep_rate are REQUIRED reporting items, all H1-H6 are explicitly hypotheses, and generalization claims are scoped to the tested datasets, backbones, and edit-validity constraints - novel domains and unconstrained edit spaces are outside the validated scope. (5) Dual-use: a calibrated margin field also tells an adversary the minimal edit to evade detection; we therefore treat m_cal as sensitive and discuss robust-margin training (adversarial flip-radius) as required follow-up before security deployment.

---
